text: generating root ideas from the seed set…

receipts.quote: right-looking is best for small n (needs parallelism) while left-looking wins for n>=512 large matrices
merge panel factorization (potf2) and TRSM into one kernel to eliminate CUDA launch overhead for small tasks
blocked (block_size=16, 128 threads/block) tiled Cholesky reference

node.text: Shape-adaptive dispatch: one-kernel-per-block for tiny n, blocked right-looking for mid, panel-pipelined for huge
node.detail: The geomean ranking rewards being good everywhere, so build a shape router keyed on (batch, n). For n<=128 (batch 4096..256): assign one matrix per threadblock, load the whole matrix into shared memory (32x32 fp32 = 4KB up to 128x128 = 64KB, fits B200 shared mem), run an unblocked column-by-column Cholesky entirely in registers/shared, one CUDA launch for the whole batch — zero per-matrix launch overhead. For 256<=n<=1024: blocked right-looking (small n needs parallelism) with block_size~16-32, still one matrix per block or a few blocks cooperating. For n>=2048 single/low-batch: switch to left-looking blocked with a separate panel-factor kernel + cuBLAS SYRK/GEMM trailing update. The dongarra fused-panel trick eliminates launch overhead where it dominates; the ICL empirical rule (right-looking small, left-looking large) sets the crossover. Selection is compile-time-templated on n buckets to avoid runtime branching cost.

receipts.quote: cuSOLVERDx exposes a __device__ POTRF that can be fused inside a single custom kernel/threadblock
do the entire factorization GPU-only — directly the fused-kernel design for the batched entries
classify each benchmark entry as memory-bound (many tiny matrices) vs compute-bound

node.text: Fully-fused single-kernel batched Cholesky for the small-n entries via cuSOLVERDx device POTRF
node.detail: For {4096,32},{1024,64},{256,128},{64,256} the bottleneck is entirely launch/dispatch overhead and memory movement, not FLOPs. Use cuSOLVERDx's __device__ POTRF fused inside one custom kernel: each threadblock (or warp for the tiniest) factors one matrix start-to-finish in shared memory, no intermediate global round-trips, single kernel launch for the batch. Compare head-to-head against cuSOLVER's batched potrfBatched to measure the launch-overhead win. Key tuning: pick threads/block and shared-mem tiling so occupancy hides the coalesced load of A. Since matrices are guaranteed SPD up to FP32 roundoff, skip pivoting/failure paths entirely. Only lower triangle needs to be read/written — exploit symmetry to halve global traffic (the 16MiB footprint entries are memory-bound).

receipts.quote: software pipelining: overlap mem copying with compute to hide latency... applicable everywhere
left-looking wins for n>=512 large matrices
the active cholesky leaderboard runs on B200 with the top single entry at ~2041us

node.text: Software-pipelined panel + trailing-update for the huge single-matrix entries (n=8192..32768)
node.detail: The n=16384/32768 single-matrix entries are compute-bound and dominate raw FLOP count; here the win is a right/left-looking blocked Cholesky where the panel factorization overlaps with the previous trailing-matrix SYRK/GEMM via double-buffered async copies (cp.async on Blackwell). Apply the Colfax/CUTLASS software-pipelining pattern to overlap the load of the next panel with the compute of the current trailing update, hiding memory latency. Trailing updates are fp32 SYRK — call tuned cuBLAS or a custom CUTLASS SYRK; the panel (a tall skinny Cholesky) is the serial bottleneck so make it a fused shared-memory kernel. Block size ~256-512 balances panel cost vs GEMM efficiency at n=32768. Since no tensor-core fp32 path helps much, rely on FMA throughput and perfect overlap.

receipts.quote: Inputs cover dense covariance-like matrices, planted spectra, diagonal matrices, damped low-rank matrices, scaled rows and columns, and tridiagonal SPD matrices
Correctness is property-based rather than elementwise comparison with one library implementation

node.text: Exploit structured input families (diagonal, tridiagonal, low-rank) with specialized fast paths
node.detail: The checker is property-based (residual + lower-triangular + positive diagonal), not elementwise vs a reference — so any valid L passes. Inputs explicitly include diagonal matrices, tridiagonal SPD, and damped low-rank. A cheap structure detector per matrix (or per batch, since cond is deterministic) can route: diagonal -> L = sqrt(diag), O(n) instead of O(n^3); tridiagonal -> banded Cholesky, O(n) with a scalar recurrence; general -> full kernel. Detection cost must be tiny (check off-band elements are ~0 via a reduction). Risk: mixed batches defeat per-batch routing and detection overhead can exceed savings for dense cases, so gate on cheap structural fingerprints. Because ranking is geomean across ALL entries, even winning a subset of shapes shifts the mean. This is the highest-leverage 'read the problem' move that a generic library implementation won't do.

receipts.quote: shared-memory budget analysis (n×ib+ib^2 elements) and the panel-thread-decay inefficiency, giving concrete autotuning knobs (block size ib=8)
a roofline/bottleneck lens helps classify each benchmark entry as memory-bound vs compute-bound

node.text: Shared-memory-budget-driven autotuning of block size ib per (n,batch) bucket
node.detail: The batched-LU/Cholesky case study gives the concrete shared-memory constraint: a panel needs n*ib + ib^2 elements, and there's a panel-thread-decay inefficiency as columns finish. Turn this into an offline autotune sweep: for each benchmark bucket, sweep ib (8,16,32,64), threads/block, and matrices-per-block, measure on the target B200, and hardcode the winner per bucket via templates. The roofline/bottleneck lens classifies each bucket as memory- or compute-bound to prune the search space (memory-bound tiny-n cares about coalescing and occupancy; compute-bound huge-n cares about GEMM efficiency and overlap). Ship a small dispatch table. This is the 'ideas-shipped = ideas-had' execution move — mechanical but reliably squeezes geomean.

receipts.quote: Paired high-batch entries at n=512..4096 separately exercise throughput and batch parallelism
If you program on A100 or newer GPUs and you don't use pipelining, you are under-utilizing your expensive GPUs

node.text: High-batch/mid-n entries: grid-stride cooperative-groups blocked Cholesky with stream overlap
node.detail: The paired high-batch entries ({640,512},{60,1024},{8,2048},{2,4096}) separately stress throughput and batch parallelism — these are the awkward middle where neither pure one-block-per-matrix (too little parallelism per matrix wastes SMs at low batch) nor pure single-matrix pipelining (batch left on table) is optimal. Use cooperative groups so several threadblocks collaborate on one matrix's blocked factorization while multiple matrices run concurrently across the grid, filling the B200's ~150+ SMs. Overlap independent matrices across CUDA streams to hide the serial panel step of one matrix behind the trailing update of another. Load-balance: choose blocks-per-matrix = SM_count / batch, clamped. This directly targets the '640,512' and '60,1024' entries that a naive batched library call underutilizes.

dispatch | Modular
IAAT: A Input-Aware Adaptive Tuning framework for Small GEMM

The shape-adaptive dispatch idea is the correct meta-strategy for this leaderboard: geomean ranking rewards being good across all 15 entries, and no single kernel wins across the 32x32-through-32768x32768 span. The fresh
node.detail: Crossover thresholds (n=128, n=1024, n=2048) are asserted from CPU/older-GPU literature and may not transfer to B200 FP32; picking them wrong loses geomean on boundary buckets. The paired high-batch entries (n=512..4096) sit exactly at bucket boundaries and may need their own regime rather than sharing with low-batch. Router adds no value if leaf kernels underperform; it is only as good as its slowest-dispatched bucket. Runtime dispatch cost is negligible but the combinatorial testing/tuning surface is large.

receipts.quote: Phase-2 ablation showed regime classifier picks suboptimal (mma_bn, cta_group) here; all regimes converge on cta_group=2, stages=6
ICL empirical rule (right-looking small, left-looking large) sets the crossover

node.text: Ablation-tuned crossover table with a boundary regime for the paired high-batch n=512..4096 entries
node.detail: Rather than assuming ICL crossovers transfer, build an explicit B200 tuning table exactly like Modular's SM100 dispatch: enumerate the 15 benchmark (batch,n) points, run each through all 3 candidate kernels, and record the winner per cell in a comptime lookup. Critically, treat the paired high-batch entries {640,512},{60,1024},{8,2048},{2,4096} as their own regime — they have enough batch parallelism to prefer per-block/cooperative kernels even at n where low-batch would go panel-pipelined. This directly mirrors the '(N=512,K=7168) shape-specific 2-branch override' where the regime classifier picked suboptimal configs and needed a hand-override.

receipts.quote: the data-parallel policy emerges as the optimal configuration for ~87% of the GEMM problem sizes... as we expand the acceptable performance slow-down margin from 5% to 20%... from approximately ~60% to ~97.6%

node.text: Tolerance-banded single-kernel fallback to shrink the dispatch surface
node.detail: Stream-K++ found data-parallel wins ~87% of shapes but Stream-K stays within 5-20% on ~97%. Apply the analogous insight: instead of 3 fully separate optimized kernels, measure whether one robust blocked kernel (e.g. the mid-n right-looking one) stays within a small slowdown band across most buckets, and only build bespoke kernels for the buckets where it loses by >X%. This minimizes engineering/testing surface — you ship 1 good kernel plus 1-2 targeted overrides (tiny-n fused, huge-n panel) rather than maintaining a full 3-way matrix, matching the builder ethos of ideas-shipped over ideas-had.

receipts.quote: the costs of boundary processing are high and cannot be neglected. To reduce or eliminate boundary processing... IAAT auto-generates hundreds of kernels of different sizes to remove pack operations
comptime DECODE_AVG_M = 8

node.text: Install-time kernel autogeneration for the discrete n-bucket set (32,64,128,256,...)
node.detail: Because the benchmark n values are a fixed discrete set of powers of two, adopt IAAT's two-stage model: at install/compile time auto-generate a fully-unrolled, boundary-free unblocked Cholesky kernel specialized per exact n for the tiny-n bucket (n=32,64,128), eliminating all runtime bounds checks and enabling full register/shared-mem residence tuned to that n. This removes the 'frequent boundary processing' cost that IAAT identifies as the small-GEMM bottleneck, and the fixed benchmark grid means the generation set is tiny (no need for hundreds of kernels).

Non-Delayed Cholesky Factorization

This is the strongest, most concrete move for the small-n leaderboard entries and the fresh evidence directly validates it. cuSOLVERDx explicitly ships a device POTRF (`__device__ void execute(data_type* A, status_type*
node.detail: cuSOLVERDx is a header/JIT MathDx dependency that may not be permitted or easy to build in the leaderboard harness; register/shared-mem pressure of unblocked POTRF may cap occupancy and negate the launch-overhead win at n=256; symmetry-halved global traffic complicates coalescing and may not pay off; correctness must still clear the reconstruction-residual checker without pivoting on near-singular cond values.

receipts.quote: potrf_batched_thread.cu ... Block and thread execution variants are provided where applicable.
Solver::suggested_batches_per_block ... Recommended optimal batches per block for a Solver description.

node.text: Warp-per-matrix vs block-per-matrix crossover using suggested_batches_per_block for n=32/64
node.detail: For the two tiniest entries ({4096,32},{1024,64}) a full threadblock per 32x32 matrix wastes threads. Use cuSOLVERDx's `BatchesPerBlock<suggested_batches_per_block>` trait to pack multiple matrices per block (the thread-execution `potrf_batched_thread.cu` variant), then sweep the suggested vs hand-tuned batches-per-block and `suggested_block_dim` to maximize matrices resident per SM. Measure occupancy-limited throughput against block-per-matrix to find the n-threshold where dedicating a whole block starts winning.

receipts.quote: For lower fill mode, only the lower triangular part of A is processed, and replaced by the lower triangular Cholesky factor L.

node.text: Fallback fused kernel without cuSOLVERDx: hand-written unblocked shared-mem POTRF if MathDx unavailable in harness
node.detail: De-risk the header dependency: implement the same single-launch, batch-per-block fusion with a hand-rolled unblocked right-looking Cholesky in shared memory (column-by-column: sqrt diagonal, scale sub-column, rank-1 trailing update), lower-triangle-only, no pivoting. This reproduces the fused-kernel data-movement win independent of whether cuSOLVERDx compiles in the submission environment, and gives a clean A/B against the cuSOLVERDx device POTRF where it is available.

receipts.quote: each thread block assumes lifetime ownership of a single matrix tile, performing all updates and the final factorization in-place using on-chip memory. A lightweight, asynchronous dependency-tracking mechanism coordinates thousands of blocks without global barriers

node.text: Persistent tile-ownership single-kernel design imported from Non-Delayed Cholesky for the n=256 batch entry
node.detail: At n=256 the unblocked device POTRF hits register/shared-mem pressure. Borrow the Non-Delayed Cholesky insight — tile-centric persistent kernel where each block owns one tile for its lifetime and trailing updates run GEMM-style with async dependency tracking instead of coarse global barriers — but scoped to intra-matrix tiles of a single 256x256 factorization while still batching 64 matrices across the grid. This converts the GEMM efficiency argument (>90% conversion) into the mid-small regime where the pure unblocked fusion runs out of on-chip budget.

node.text: [Perf] Add tuned Triton fused MoE configs for NVIDIA H20
CarrotSwordsman/H20-LLM-Cookbook
reports/2026-06_h20_moe_tuning.md
[Paper Note] A Paired Testing Protocol for Batch-Conditioned Refusal Robustness
[2404.12268] How Population Diversity Influences the Efficiency of Crossover
Structure Over Scale: Rethinking Adaptation for Reinforcement Learning with Veri

The core move — an explicit per-cell tuning table on B200 rather than trusting theoretical ICL crossovers to transfer — is sound and well-supported by the fresh vLLM/H20 evidence, which empirically demonstrates that a co
node.detail: Single-shot tuning noise can pick a config that loses to a robust default (H20 shows 0.73x); need multi-run medians per cell. The table only helps if the 3 candidate kernels are individually strong — it's a multiplier on others' work, not standalone value. B200/SM100 access for tuning may be unavailable in-harness, forcing offline-baked constants that could be wrong for the actual eval GPU. Comptime lookup over exactly 15 cells risks overfitting to benchmark shapes with no generalization, though the objective's fixed grid makes this acceptable.

receipts.quote: the chosen "best" config at one grid point happens to be slightly worse than the default's hardcoded fallback at adjacent shapes
worst 0.73× at E=64, N=2560, batch=512 ... look like single-shot autotuning artefacts on the sparse grid

node.text: Robust per-cell selection with median-of-k timing and tie-band fallback to a safe default kernel
node.detail: For each of the 15 (batch,n) cells, run all 3 candidate kernels k times, take the median runtime, and only override the safe-default kernel if the winner beats it by more than a noise margin (e.g. >5%). Within the tie-band, keep the most robust kernel to avoid the H20-observed 0.73x single-shot autotuning regression. This turns the comptime lookup into a confidence-gated table rather than raw argmin.

receipts.quote: Large batches (≥2048) tie with the default within noise, as those workloads are HBM-bandwidth-bound and the kernel config has limited leverage

node.text: Batch-parallelism scalar as the regime axis instead of n-buckets: route on batch*n rather than n alone
node.detail: Replace the n-only comptime bucketing with a router keyed on a batch-parallelism proxy (e.g. total blocks = batch times blocks-per-matrix vs SM count on B200). The paired high-batch cells {640,512},{60,1024},{8,2048},{2,4096} have enough independent work to saturate SMs with per-block/cooperative kernels even at n where a single low-batch matrix would starve them and need panel-pipelining. Make occupancy-saturation the explicit dispatch predicate so the two n=512..4096 regimes fall out mechanically rather than by hardcoded exception.

This is a well-scoped, mechanically sound micro-optimization targeting exactly the two entries where the parent fused-kernel approach leaves the most on the table: {4096,32} and {1024,64}, where a 32x32 matrix underuses
node.detail: (1) Only moves 2 of 15 geomean entries, so even a large per-entry win is diluted. (2) Heavy overlap with the existing sibling 'Ablation-tuned crossover table' and 'Shape-adaptive dispatch' — risk of duplicating tuning work. (3) At n=32/64 the entries are memory-bound (16MiB footprint), so occupancy packing may hit a bandwidth wall before threads-per-matrix stops mattering, making the crossover less about compute utilization than the framing assumes. (4) cuSOLVERDx thread-execution variant correctness/perf at these sizes is unverified in the harness; suggested_batches_per_block may not be near-optimal and hand-tuning may yield little over the suggested default.

receipts.quote: when the blocking size is (128 × 128 × 64), both DRAM and shared memory will throttle the program
One good clue that you're missing an option is when you see a sudden drop in achieved TFLOP/s for particular shape.

node.text: Bandwidth-roofline model to predict the warp/block crossover before sweeping
node.detail: Rather than pure empirical sweep, build a two-term cost model per (n,batch): coalesced load of the lower triangle (n(n+1)/2 FP32 = the 16MiB-bound term) vs the O(n^3/3) FLOP term executed by k matrices-per-block. Use the roofline logic from the HKUST tensor-core paper (DRAM+shared-mem throttle points) to predict where added batches-per-block stop improving throughput because DRAM saturates. This tells you the crossover n analytically and reduces the sweep to a 2-3 point confirmation, saving tuning budget. At n=32/64 the model likely shows the entries are DRAM-bound, meaning maximize resident batches until bandwidth saturates, not until threads-per-matrix is 'full'.

receipts.quote: it allows 2 CTAs to reside on one SM, which makes the warps run in an asynchronous manner. The asynchronous behavior gives the warp scheduler more opportunities to switch to the other warps for increased throughput.

node.text: 2-CTA-per-SM asynchrony vs single-fat-block for the tiny-n packed variant
node.detail: Borrow the HKUST finding that a smaller block allowing 2 CTAs resident per SM gives the warp scheduler more cross-CTA switching opportunity and higher throughput than one large block. Concretely: for {4096,32}, compare (a) one big block packing many 32x32 matrices via suggested_batches_per_block against (b) deliberately smaller blocks sized so >=2 blocks co-reside per SM, letting independent-matrix warps hide each other's shared-mem-load latency. Measure whether the async scheduling win beats the shared-memory-reuse win — the same tradeoff cuBLAS's 128x128 choice exploited over 256x256.

text: expansion 5/6: deep-diving "Software-pipelined panel + trailing-update for the huge single-matrix "

node.text: High Accuracy Matrix Computations on Neural Engines: a Study of QR Factorization
4282d09 [MPS] Migrate lu_factor to Metal kernels (#187038)
Efficient GEMM Kernel Designs with Pipelining | SIGARCH
4282d09 [MPS] Migrate lu_factor to Metal kernels (#187038)
High Accuracy Matrix Computations on Neural Engines: a Study of QR Factorization
fishmingyu/qrv2-gpu-mode

The core insight — blocked right/left-looking Cholesky with panel/trailing-update decomposition where GEMM dominates FLOPs and panel is the latency bottleneck — is exactly right for the huge single-matrix entries, and th
node.detail: (1) cuSOLVER/cuBLAS batched+single POTRF is already highly tuned for large n; beating it by hand is very hard and a custom pipeline may only match it. (2) FP32 has no tensor-core shortcut, removing the main modern win. (3) These huge entries are only 3/15 cells in a geomean — limited ranking impact. (4) A fused shared-memory panel Cholesky at block 256-512 is nontrivial to get numerically robust at the checker's residual tolerance. (5) Blackwell TMA/cp.async pipelining is complex to implement correctly under a time budget vs. just wrapping cuSOLVER.

receipts.quote: the overall performance of the accelerated blocked Householder QR is no better than cuSolver SGEQ[RF]
the GEMM execution is completely or partially overlapped by the panel, thus speeding up GEMM has only limited effect on the overall QR speed

node.text: Measure cuSOLVER potrf vs hand-pipelined on n=8192..32768 first; only build custom if >1.3x headroom
node.detail: Before investing in a CUTLASS pipeline, baseline: (a) single cusolverDnSpotrf per matrix, (b) cuSOLVER with cusolverDnXpotrf 64-bit API, (c) manual blocked loop calling cuBLAS SSYRK + STRSM + recursive potrf on panels. Profile achieved GB/s and % of FMA peak. The QR paper showed a hand-blocked Householder was 'no better than cuSolver SGEQRF' — the analogous risk here is that cuSOLVER potrf already saturates. Proceed to the custom pipelined kernel only if a gap >1.3x exists at n=16384/32768. This gates the expensive work on evidence.

receipts.quote: A blocked, right-looking LU with partial pivoting... each block is factored as 32-wide register sub-panels, then the trailing submatrix is updated
gemmLU / gemmSimdLU | sgemm | Schur-complement trailing update A22 -= L21*U12

node.text: Right-looking blocked Cholesky with recursive panel factorization, ported from the MPS LU kernel decomposition
node.detail: Adapt the pytorch MPS lu_factor structure directly to Cholesky: replace partial-pivot panel factor with unblocked shared-mem POTF2 (Cholesky needs no pivoting for SPD), keep the trsm (STRSM on the block row L21) and the Schur trailing update as SSYRK/SGEMM (A22 -= L21 L21^T). Use the MPS commit's register-resident panel trick (each thread owns R rows, width W=32/R) for the panel, and recurse the panel itself for block width >512. Block-column walk over NBo-wide panels with in-place factorization. This gives a concrete, validated kernel DAG rather than a from-scratch design.

receipts.quote: PipelineTmaAsync, to create a 2-stage pipeline used in a Hopper GEMM kernel that overlaps TMA with WGMMA
pipeline.producer_acquire(smem_pipe_write)... copy(tma_load_a.with(*tmaBar, 0)... pipeline.consumer_wait(smem_pipe_read)... gemm(tiled_mma...

node.text: 2-stage PipelineTmaAsync double-buffer overlapping next-panel POTF2+TRSM with current SSYRK trailing update
node.detail: Apply the exact CUTLASS 2-stage producer/consumer pipeline from the SIGARCH blog to Cholesky: the producer async-loads the next block column (cp.async on Ampere / TMA on Hopper-Blackwell) into a double-buffered smem stage while the consumer runs the current trailing SSYRK. Structure the loop as the blog's producer_acquire/copy → consumer_wait/gemm/consumer_release, but with the panel POTF2+TRSM as the serial dependency that must complete before the trailing update it feeds. Since the panel is the critical-path serializer, prefetch the next panel's data during the current (larger) trailing SYRK to hide its latency. Tune stage depth and block size 256-512 empirically per the QR paper's B-tuning guidance.

text: expansion 6/6: deep-diving "Exploit structured input families (diagonal, tridiagonal, low-rank) wi"

node.text: Exploiting Compositional Structure for Automatic and Efficient Numerical Linear
ENH: `linalg.solve`: detect and exploit matrix structure · Pull Request #12824 ·
A basic linear algebra compiler for structured matrices
Exploiting Compositional Structure for Automatic and Efficient Numerical Linear
Harnessing Batched BLAS/LAPACK Kernels on GPUs for Parallel Solutions of Block T
Sketching Low-Rank Plus Diagonal Matrices

The core insight — that a property-based checker rewards ANY valid L, so structured inputs (diagonal, tridiagonal, low-rank) admit O(n) or O(n·rank) fast paths that a generic library POTRF never takes — is genuinely leve
node.detail: Detection overhead can exceed savings on dense cells (SciPy: 24% structure-detection). Mixed-family batches within one cell defeat per-batch routing and force divergent per-matrix branches. Structured cells are few and already cheap, capping absolute gain. Fast-path L must still satisfy positive-diagonal + residual tolerance under FP32 (e.g. tridiagonal scalar recurrence sequential dependency).

receipts.quote: find matrix structure: 24%
we could speed up the finiteness check, especially for the diagonal and tridiagonal cases, by checking only the elements that will be used in the calculation

node.text: Fused detect-and-factor: fold band-structure fingerprint into the first kernel pass, zero extra launches
node.detail: Instead of a separate detection reduction, compute a cheap off-band mass fingerprint (sum of |A[i,j]| for |i-j|>1 vs diagonal mass) inside the SAME kernel that begins loading tiles for the general blocked Cholesky. If the fingerprint (a single warp-reduce over the already-loaded shared tile) says diagonal or tridiagonal, the kernel writes L=sqrt(diag) or runs the Thomas-style banded recurrence and exits early; otherwise it proceeds to full POTRF with the tiles already resident. This amortizes detection against the load the dense path needs anyway, directly answering the SciPy '24% structure detection' overhead problem by never doing a standalone scan.

receipts.quote: these schemes are inherently sequential, with each block depending on its predecessor, and a naive GPU implementation that iterates over blocks issues many small BLAS and LAPACK calls
solve[PSD[Tridiagonal]]

node.text: Batched banded Cholesky kernel specialized for the tridiagonal SPD family (one thread-block per group of matrices)
node.detail: For cells where the tridiagonal family dominates, run a dedicated batched banded Cholesky: L has only main + first subdiagonal, computed by the scalar recurrence L[i,i]=sqrt(a_ii - L[i,i-1]^2), L[i+1,i]=a_{i+1,i}/L[i,i]. The recurrence is sequential in i but embarrassingly parallel across the batch, so assign one thread per matrix and stride over the batch — turning the sequential dependency (flagged as the GPU pain point for Thomas-style solvers) into a non-issue because parallelism comes from batch width, not within-matrix. Write zeros to the strict lower band. Targets the n=32..1024 low-batch and high-batch tridiagonal inputs where O(n) beats O(n^3) by orders of magnitude.

receipts.quote: The main use for polyalgorithm is to work automagically regardless whether the user knows it or not. So if they pass a bidiagonal array the machine should recognize it.
If a specific LinearOperator subclass does not have a specific solve dispatch rule then we default to the base-case solve rule using GMRES

node.text: Cheap per-cell structural fingerprint precomputed once, routing the whole (batch,n) cell before any factorization launch
node.detail: Since cond is deterministic and families may be homogeneous within a cell, run a single fused reduction over the entire batch that computes three scalars: max off-tridiagonal-band relative mass, diagonal-only relative mass, and (via a few random probe MVMs) an estimate of effective rank for the damped-low-rank family. If the whole cell fingerprints as one family, dispatch the specialized kernel for the entire cell (no per-matrix divergence); if heterogeneous, fall back to the general blocked kernel for the whole cell. This gates on 'cheap structural fingerprints' exactly as the parent proposes but resolves the mixed-batch risk by making routing all-or-nothing per cell, avoiding warp divergence from per-matrix branches.

markdown:
# Exploration Brief — GPU MODE — Batched Dense Cholesky Factorization (leaderboard 776)

> Distilled from a recursive idea tree seeded by your curation: 10 unread saves read, 10 signals scouted, 22 ideas explored (0 pruned). Every idea carries receipts back into your library or to discovered sources.

## 1. Fully-fused single-kernel batched Cholesky for the small-n entries via cuSOLVERDx device POTRF · score 8/10


For {4096,32},{1024,64},{256,128},{64,256} the bottleneck is entirely launch/dispatch overhead and memory movement, not FLOPs. Use cuSOLVERDx's __device__ POTRF fused inside one custom kernel: each threadblock (or warp for the tiniest) factors one matrix start-to-finish in shared memory, no intermediate global round-trips, single kernel launch for the batch. Compare head-to-head against cuSOLVER's batched potrfBatched to measure the launch-overhead win. Key tuning: pick threads/block and shared-mem tiling so occupancy hides the coalesced load of A. Since matrices are guaranteed SPD up to FP32 roundoff, skip pivoting/failure paths entirely. Only lower triangle needs to be read/written — exploit symmetry to halve global traffic (the 16MiB footprint entries are memory-bound).

**Deep-dive verdict.** This is the strongest, most concrete move for the small-n leaderboard entries and the fresh evidence directly validates it. cuSOLVERDx explicitly ships a device POTRF (`__device__ void execute(data_type* A, status_type*  Risks: cuSOLVERDx is a header/JIT MathDx dependency that may not be permitted or easy to build in the leaderboard harness; register/shared-mem pressure of unblocked POTRF may cap occupancy and negate the launch-overhead win at n=256; symmetry-halved global traffic complicates coalescing and may not pay off; correctness must still clear the reconstruction-residual checker without pivoting on near-singular cond values.

**Receipts:** [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/get_started/potrf.html] ("cuSOLVERDx exposes a __device__ POTRF that can be fused inside a single custom k"); [https://www.netlib.org/utk/people/JackDongarra/PAPERS/fast-batch-2014.pdf] ("do the entire factorization GPU-only — directly the fused-kernel design for the "); [doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6] ("classify each benchmark entry as memory-bound (many tiny matrices) vs compute-bo")

## 2. Shape-adaptive dispatch: one-kernel-per-block for tiny n, blocked right-looking for mid, panel-pipelined for huge · score 7/10


The geomean ranking rewards being good everywhere, so build a shape router keyed on (batch, n). For n<=128 (batch 4096..256): assign one matrix per threadblock, load the whole matrix into shared memory (32x32 fp32 = 4KB up to 128x128 = 64KB, fits B200 shared mem), run an unblocked column-by-column Cholesky entirely in registers/shared, one CUDA launch for the whole batch — zero per-matrix launch overhead. For 256<=n<=1024: blocked right-looking (small n needs parallelism) with block_size~16-32, still one matrix per block or a few blocks cooperating. For n>=2048 single/low-batch: switch to left-looking blocked with a separate panel-factor kernel + cuBLAS SYRK/GEMM trailing update. The dongarra fused-panel trick eliminates launch overhead where it dominates; the ICL empirical rule (right-looking small, left-looking large) sets the crossover. Selection is compile-time-templated on n buckets to avoid runtime branching cost.

**Deep-dive verdict.** The shape-adaptive dispatch idea is the correct meta-strategy for this leaderboard: geomean ranking rewards being good across all 15 entries, and no single kernel wins across the 32x32-through-32768x32768 span. The fresh Risks: Crossover thresholds (n=128, n=1024, n=2048) are asserted from CPU/older-GPU literature and may not transfer to B200 FP32; picking them wrong loses geomean on boundary buckets. The paired high-batch entries (n=512..4096) sit exactly at bucket boundaries and may need their own regime rather than sharing with low-batch. Router adds no value if leaf kernels underperform; it is only as good as its slowest-dispatched bucket. Runtime dispatch cost is negligible but the combinatorial testing/tuning surface is large.

**Receipts:** [https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2017/icl-utk-987-2017.pdf] ("right-looking is best for small n (needs parallelism) while left-looking wins fo"); [https://www.netlib.org/utk/people/JackDongarra/PAPERS/fast-batch-2014.pdf] ("merge panel factorization (potf2) and TRSM into one kernel to eliminate CUDA lau"); [https://github.com/NVIDIA/warp/blob/7ddcbbb2/warp/examples/tile/example_tile_block_cholesky.py] ("blocked (block_size=16, 128 threads/block) tiled Cholesky reference")

## 3. Install-time kernel autogeneration for the discrete n-bucket set (32,64,128,256,...) · score 7/10

*Lineage: Shape-adaptive dispatch: one-kernel-per-block for tiny n, blocked right-looking for mid, panel-pipelined for huge → this*

Because the benchmark n values are a fixed discrete set of powers of two, adopt IAAT's two-stage model: at install/compile time auto-generate a fully-unrolled, boundary-free unblocked Cholesky kernel specialized per exact n for the tiny-n bucket (n=32,64,128), eliminating all runtime bounds checks and enabling full register/shared-mem residence tuned to that n. This removes the 'frequent boundary processing' cost that IAAT identifies as the small-GEMM bottleneck, and the fixed benchmark grid means the generation set is tiny (no need for hundreds of kernels).

**Receipts:** [https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2208.09822] ("the costs of boundary processing are high and cannot be neglected. To reduce or "); [https://docs.modular.com/max/api/kernels/linalg/matmul/gpu/sm100_structured/grouped_block_scaled_1d1d/dispatch/] ("comptime DECODE_AVG_M = 8")

## 4. Fallback fused kernel without cuSOLVERDx: hand-written unblocked shared-mem POTRF if MathDx unavailable in harness · score 7/10

*Lineage: Fully-fused single-kernel batched Cholesky for the small-n entries via cuSOLVERDx device POTRF → this*

De-risk the header dependency: implement the same single-launch, batch-per-block fusion with a hand-rolled unblocked right-looking Cholesky in shared memory (column-by-column: sqrt diagonal, scale sub-column, rank-1 trailing update), lower-triangle-only, no pivoting. This reproduces the fused-kernel data-movement win independent of whether cuSOLVERDx compiles in the submission environment, and gives a clean A/B against the cuSOLVERDx device POTRF where it is available.

**Receipts:** [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/get_started/functions/potrf.html] ("For lower fill mode, only the lower triangular part of A is processed, and repla"); [https://www.netlib.org/utk/people/JackDongarra/PAPERS/fast-batch-2014.pdf] ("")

## 5. Persistent tile-ownership single-kernel design imported from Non-Delayed Cholesky for the n=256 batch entry · score 7/10

*Lineage: Fully-fused single-kernel batched Cholesky for the small-n entries via cuSOLVERDx device POTRF → this*

At n=256 the unblocked device POTRF hits register/shared-mem pressure. Borrow the Non-Delayed Cholesky insight — tile-centric persistent kernel where each block owns one tile for its lifetime and trailing updates run GEMM-style with async dependency tracking instead of coarse global barriers — but scoped to intra-matrix tiles of a single 256x256 factorization while still batching 64 matrices across the grid. This converts the GEMM efficiency argument (>90% conversion) into the mid-small regime where the pure unblocked fusion runs out of on-chip budget.

**Receipts:** [https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3797905.3800554] ("each thread block assumes lifetime ownership of a single matrix tile, performing")

## 6. Robust per-cell selection with median-of-k timing and tie-band fallback to a safe default kernel · score 7/10

*Lineage: Shape-adaptive dispatch: one-kernel-per-block for tiny n, blocked right-looking for mid, panel-pipelined for huge → Ablation-tuned crossover table with a boundary regime for the paired high-batch n=512..4096 entries → this*

For each of the 15 (batch,n) cells, run all 3 candidate kernels k times, take the median runtime, and only override the safe-default kernel if the winner beats it by more than a noise margin (e.g. >5%). Within the tie-band, keep the most robust kernel to avoid the H20-observed 0.73x single-shot autotuning regression. This turns the comptime lookup into a confidence-gated table rather than raw argmin.

**Receipts:** [https://github.com/CarrotSwordsman/H20-LLM-Cookbook/blob/main/reports/2026-06_h20_moe_tuning.md] ("the chosen "best" config at one grid point happens to be slightly worse than the"); [https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/44152] ("worst 0.73× at E=64, N=2560, batch=512 ... look like single-shot autotuning arte")
